title: Te Kōrero o te Awa: The Living Story of Our River / Ngā Kōrero Tuku Iho o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the pūrākau (traditional stories) and mātauranga Māori associated with Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. This awa is a living ancestor with deep connections to Ngāti Kahungunu history, containing stories of taniwha, ancestral journeys, and traditional food gathering practices.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three traditional stories (pūrākau) associated with Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate te reo Māori vocabulary
Construct and deliver a simple mihi that acknowledges the awa as a significant feature of our local environment and cultural identity
Create a visual narrative (storyboard or digital presentation) that retells one pūrākau of the awa using te reo Māori phrases and sentences
Participate in a guided visit to the awa, demonstrating respectful tikanga and using location-specific vocabulary to describe features observed

activities.title: He Mihi mō te Awa - Honouring Our River
activities.description: Students learn and practice a collective mihi that acknowledges Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu, and our responsibility as kaitiaki. Includes learning specific vocabulary: awa (river), mauri (life force), kaitiaki (guardian), and local place names.

activities.title: Ngā Pūrākau o te Whenua - Stories of the Land
activities.description: Working with local kaumātua, students explore traditional stories of the awa, including the taniwha protector stories and ancestral journeys. Students create illustrated story maps using te reo Māori captions and labels for geographical features.

activities.title: Te Haerenga ki te Awa - Journey to the River
activities.description: Guided visit to specific sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku where students practice observational language (he aha tēnei? - what is this?), record natural sounds, and document traditional food gathering sites using te reo Māori terminology.

activities.title: He Kōrero Ataata - Digital Storytelling
activities.description: Students work in small groups to create short digital stories retelling one pūrākau of the awa, incorporating te reo Māori narration, traditional waiata, and visual elements that show respect for the stories and the environment.

resources: Local kaumātua and knowledge holders from Ngāti Kahungunu
"Ngā Pūrākau o Te Matau a Māui" - Stories of Hawke's Bay resource book
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku historical maps and aerial photographs from Hastings District Council archives
Digital recording equipment and storyboarding templates from college media department

tasks: Oral presentation of mihi acknowledging the awa
Completed illustrated story map with te reo Māori labels
Digital story retelling a pūrākau with te reo Māori narration
Participation observation during awa visit demonstrating tikanga

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic te reo Māori vocabulary, formulaic expressions, and simple sentences
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Level 4: Recognise that te reo Māori is the language of the tangata whenua of Aotearoa New Zealand
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways
